narrative_blocks.header: Married the Million Dollar Cinderella; Harvard 1905 stockbroker
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Neilson Hinckley married Catherine Livingston Hamersley — 'Kitty,' New York's Million Dollar Cinderella, who had grown up believing she was merely comfortably situated until she inherited a million dollars at twenty-one — at Grace Church in Manhattan on October 22, 1914, the match described by newspaper coverage as 'of widespread social importance'; their son Samuel Neilson Hinckley Jr. was born September 14, 1915. They divorced before 1922, and Hinckley — a Harvard Class of 1905 stockbroker who had once been the husband of the most famous heiress in New York — died at forty-nine in February 1931, leaving their son to carry the Hamersley name forward through his own grandchildren, one of whom was named Hugo Hamersley Livingston Beekman.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Million Dollar Cinderella; Harvard 1905 stockbroker

They divorced before 1922, and Hinckley — a Harvard Class of 1905 stockbroker who had once been the husband of the most famous heiress in New York — died at forty-nine in February 1931, leaving their son to carry the Hamersley name forward through his own grandchildren, one of whom was named Hugo Hamersley Livingston Beekman.

burial.plot_notes: Trinity Churchyard Financial District per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Catherine Livingston' after her great-grandmother Catherine Livingston Hooker Hamersley (H02030, 1817-1867), who was herself named for the Livingston family connection through her mother Sarah Helen Reade Hooker. The name traveled through three generations in the Hamersley family.

narrative_blocks.header: New York called her the Million Dollar Cinderella
narrative_blocks.body: By his will, J. Hooker Hamersley arranged that his daughter Catherine — 'Kitty' — would grow up ignorant of her fortune, raised by guardian Katherine Lowrie with a twenty-five-cent monthly allowance and plain toys until she reached twenty-one and inherited over a million dollars. Robert W.

narrative_blocks.header: Once reported engaged to Vincent Astor
narrative_blocks.body: Before her three marriages, Kitty Hamersley was reported to have been engaged to Vincent Astor — son of the Titanic victim John Jacob Astor IV — placing her in a romantic orbit that eventually brought her to Henry Coleman Drayton, who was Vincent Astor's own first cousin (both were grandchildren of William Backhouse Astor Jr. and Caroline Astor). She married Drayton in 1922 in Palm Beach, divorced him, married Charles Whitney Carpenter Jr. in 1929, divorced him, and lived until 1977 at age eighty-six — the last survivor of J. Hooker Hamersley's children.

narrative_blocks.header: Her second husband's family defined the Four Hundred
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Coleman Drayton, Kitty's second husband, was the grandson of Caroline Astor — the woman whose ballroom held exactly four hundred guests, giving the name to the American social aristocracy — and his mother Charlotte Astor's younger brother was John Jacob Astor IV, the Titanic's wealthiest passenger. His sister Caroline Astor Drayton married William Phillips, undersecretary of state under FDR and ambassador to Italy 1938-1941, making the Drayton-Astor orbit one of the densest social webs connecting the Gilded Age and the New Deal era combined.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from the last Governor of Plymouth Colony
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Neilson Hinckley Jr. was a direct descendant of Samuel Hinckley, who arrived in Scituate, Massachusetts aboard the Hercules in 1634, and of Thomas Hinckley (1618-1706), the last Governor of Plymouth Colony before it merged into Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1692 — a Pilgrim lineage that reached through the Manhattan drawing rooms of the Hamersley-Hooker family to forty years as a stockbroker in San Francisco. He was president of the Harvard Advocate at Harvard, and when he died at Villa Marin in San Rafael in July 2008, his grandson was named Hugo Hamersley Livingston Beekman — still carrying the ancestral names four generations removed from the Gilded Age.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

INST133.name: Boston Latin School
INST133.notes: America's oldest public school. In-law connection through Samuel Parker Hinckley (father of I00974; Harvard A.B. 1871; 'fitted for college at Boston Latin School'). Confirmed Hooker-tree alumni: HD2066 Frederick William Atherton and HD2304 John Noyes Colby (class of 1912). In-law connection: I00974's father Samuel Parker Hinckley (Harvard A.B. 1871). | Merged from INST059: John Noyes Colby (HD2304) class of 1912 — the largest senior class in school history to that point (102 members). Received award for excellence in modern studies Jun 4, 1908. Class Day exercises Apr 24, 1912.
